section.title: Preparations for the Temple
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 4 closed with kings from every nation traveling to hear Solomon's God-given wisdom. Chapter 5 shows that same wisdom at work in international diplomacy, as Hiram king of Tyre, long a friend of David, sends envoys to the new king. Solomon explains that David could not build a house for the Name of the LORD because of constant war, but now that the LORD has given him rest on every side, the time to build has come. Hiram gladly supplies cedar and cypress timber in exchange for food, the two kings seal a treaty, and Solomon conscripts a vast labor force to quarry stone and prepare timber, so that everything necessary is ready before a single stone of the temple itself is laid.
section.thesis: Because the LORD has given Solomon rest on every side, he is free to take up the house for the Name of the LORD that David was denied, and through a costly partnership of labor, timber, and stone with a Gentile king, Solomon prepares everything necessary before construction begins, so that even the temple's foundations testify to God's providence gathering the nations toward His house.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Solomon's message to Hiram explaining why now is the time to build, verses 1 to 6; Hiram's joyful reply and the treaty between the two kings, verses 7 to 12; and Solomon's labor force quarrying stone and preparing timber for the temple, verses 13 to 18.

units.label: Solomon's message to Hiram
units.note: rest at last, so now I will build

verses.text: Now when Hiram king of Tyre heard that Solomon had been anointed king in his father's place, he sent envoys to Solomon; for Hiram had always been a friend of David.
And Solomon relayed this message to Hiram:

“As you are well aware, due to the wars waged on all sides against my father David, he could not build a {{bayit|house}} for the {{shem|Name}} of the LORD his God until the LORD had put his enemies under his feet.
But now the LORD my God has given me {{menuchah|rest}} on every side, and there is no adversary or crisis.

So behold, I plan to build a house for the Name of the LORD my God, according to what the LORD said to my father David: ‘I will put your son on your throne in your place, and he will build the house for My Name.’

Now therefore, order that cedars of Lebanon be cut down for me. My servants will be with your servants, and I will pay your servants whatever wages you set, for you know that there are none among us as skilled in logging as the Sidonians.”

units.label: Hiram's reply and the treaty between kings
units.note: cedar and cypress for wheat and oil

verses.text: When Hiram received Solomon's message, he rejoiced greatly and said, “Blessed be the LORD this day! He has given David a wise son over this great people!”
Then Hiram sent a reply to Solomon, saying: “I have received your message; I will do all you desire regarding the cedar and cypress timber.

My servants will haul the logs from Lebanon to the Sea, and I will float them as rafts by sea to the place you specify. There I will separate the logs, and you can take them away. And in exchange, you can meet my needs by providing my household with food.”
So Hiram provided Solomon with all the cedar and cypress timber he wanted,
and year after year Solomon would provide Hiram with 20,000 cors of wheat as food for his household, as well as 20,000 baths of pure olive oil.
And the LORD gave Solomon wisdom, as He had promised him. There was peace between Hiram and Solomon, and the two of them made a {{berit|treaty}}.

units.label: Solomon's labor force
units.note: quarrying and preparing for the house of the LORD

verses.text: Then King Solomon conscripted a labor force of 30,000 men from all Israel.
He sent them to Lebanon in monthly shifts of 10,000 men, so that they would spend one month in Lebanon and two months at home. And Adoniram was in charge of the forced labor.
Solomon had 70,000 porters and 80,000 stonecutters in the mountains,
not including his 3,300 foremen who supervised the workers.
And the king commanded them to quarry large, costly stones to lay the foundation of the temple with dressed stones.
So Solomon's and Hiram's builders, along with the Gebalites, quarried the stone and prepared the timber and stone for the construction of the temple.

bayit.gloss: House, home, or temple. Solomon speaks repeatedly of building a bayit for the LORD, the same word used for an ordinary household, showing that the temple is conceived as God's dwelling place among His people rather than merely a national monument.

shem.gloss: Name. Solomon says the house is built for the Name of the LORD, not simply for the LORD directly, a Deuteronomic way of speaking, Deuteronomy 12:11, that ties the temple to God's revealed character and reputation dwelling among His people, rather than confining God Himself to a building.

menuchah.gloss: Rest, quietness from strife. David could not build because he lacked this rest, verse 3; Solomon can build precisely because the LORD has now given him menuchah on every side, verse 4, fulfilling God's word that David's son, a man of rest, would build the house, 1 Chronicles 22:9.

berit.gloss: Covenant or treaty. The BSB footnote notes that forms of this Hebrew word are elsewhere translated covenant in most passages. Hiram and Solomon's treaty of mutual provision uses the same vocabulary Scripture uses for God's covenants with His people, framing even this international agreement within the larger biblical language of binding, faithful relationship.

basic.context: As soon as Solomon's fame for wisdom spreads, Hiram king of Tyre, an old friend of David, reaches out to the new king. Solomon explains that his father could not build a house for the LORD's Name because of constant warfare, but the LORD has now given him rest on every side, so the time to build has finally come. Hiram gladly agrees to supply cedar and cypress timber in exchange for wheat and olive oil, the two kings make a treaty, and Solomon then conscripts a massive labor force, Israelites working in Lebanon on monthly rotation alongside porters and stonecutters, to quarry stone and prepare timber for the temple's foundation. The key questions this chapter raises are why rest from war was the necessary condition for building, what it means that God's house is built through partnership with a Gentile king, and how the enormous cost in labor and materials shapes our reading of what follows in chapter 6.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing genuine diplomatic and economic arrangements; it is not a manual for church building programs today. What the chapter teaches as enduring truth is that God's timing matters, rest precedes building, that God can use even a pagan king's resources and goodwill to advance His purposes, and that costly, careful preparation properly precedes any work done for the LORD.
basic.originalLanguages: Solomon speaks of building a bayit, a house, for the shem, the Name, of the LORD, a Deuteronomic phrase that ties the temple to God's presence and reputation among the nations rather than confining Him to a building. He can build now because the LORD has given him menuchah, rest, on every side, precisely the condition God had said would qualify David's son to build, 1 Chronicles 22:9. And the whole arrangement with Hiram is sealed by a berit, a treaty, the same word Scripture elsewhere reserves for covenant, its ordinary language now framing an alliance between an Israelite and a Gentile king.
basic.typology: Gentile resources, cedar from Lebanon, and Gentile labor from Tyre and Byblos, are gathered into the preparation of God's house, foreshadowing the day when the nations themselves would be gathered into the house of God, no longer merely supplying its materials but becoming living stones within it. Solomon's rest, given by the LORD as the condition for building, points to the greater rest Christ gives, in whom alone true temple building, the building of the church, can go forward.
basic.christ: Solomon can only build because the LORD has first given him rest on every side, verse 4, a rest promised beforehand as the mark of the son who would build God's house, 1 Chronicles 22:9-10. Jesus is the greater Son of David who both gives true rest, Matthew 11:28-29, and builds a greater house, His church, upon that finished rest, Matthew 16:18. And just as Gentile Hiram gladly supplies what Israel lacks so that the LORD's house can be built, the New Testament shows the nations themselves, once outsiders, being built together into a holy temple in the Lord, Ephesians 2:19-22.
basic.god: God is sovereign over international affairs, moving a foreign king to supply exactly what His temple requires, and faithful to His covenant word to David that a son of rest would build the house for His Name. He is also a God who values careful, costly, unhurried preparation, willing to spend years gathering timber, stone, and labor before the temple proper is even begun, showing that diligent preparation itself can be an act of worship.
basic.crossReferences: First Chronicles 22 records David's own preparations and the LORD's word that his son, a man of peace, would build the house. Second Samuel 7 contains the original promise that David's son would build a house for the LORD's Name. Ephesians 2 speaks of Jew and Gentile alike being built together into a holy temple in the Lord, and Matthew 11 speaks of the rest that Christ, the greater Son of David, gives His people.
basic.oneStory: David longed to build God a house but was a man of war; here his son, given rest by the LORD, gathers Gentile skill and Israelite labor to prepare for the task. This is one more step in the long story of God dwelling among His people, from the tabernacle in the wilderness toward the temple in Jerusalem, and ultimately toward Christ, the true temple, in whom God tabernacles among us and gathers people from every nation into one house built for His Name.

context.literary: The chapter unfolds as a diplomatic exchange followed by a report of labor mobilization: Solomon's message, verses 1 to 6, Hiram's warm reply and the resulting treaty, verses 7 to 12, and a summary of the workforce and quarrying, verses 13 to 18. The repeated phrase 'a house for the Name of the LORD' frames the whole chapter around a single controlling purpose, preparation for that house, before the reader ever reaches the building itself in chapter 6.
context.historical: Tyre, under Hiram, was a Phoenician maritime and trading power renowned for skilled woodworking and access to the cedar forests of Lebanon; such treaties exchanging timber and craftsmanship for agricultural produce were a normal feature of ancient Near Eastern diplomacy between resource-poor and resource-rich neighbors. Floating logs by sea as rafts was a standard method of transporting timber from Lebanon down the coast. The large labor figures, 30,000 conscripted Israelites in rotating shifts alongside 70,000 porters and 80,000 stonecutters, reflect the scale of royal building projects known from the wider ancient Near East.
context.canonical: This chapter fulfills the promise first given in 2 Samuel 7:12-13 and expanded in 1 Chronicles 22:6-10, that David's son, a man of rest, would build the house for the LORD's Name that David himself was barred from building because of his many wars and bloodshed. It also anticipates a recurring biblical pattern in which Gentile rulers and resources serve the building of God's house, a pattern that culminates in the New Testament's vision of the nations built together into the true temple.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the temple's construction begins not with the first stone laid but with God-given rest, careful diplomacy, and costly preparation, fulfilling His covenant word to David through Solomon's obedient initiative and Hiram's willing partnership.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific ancient treaty and a specific ancient labor conscription; it does not prescribe forced labor or any particular building method for God's people today. What endures is the principle that building for the LORD requires God-given rest as its foundation, wise use of available resources and relationships, and unhurried, costly preparation before the work itself begins.

debates.question: How should Solomon's conscripted labor force of verses 13 to 18 be assessed morally?

views.label: A legitimate and celebrated national mobilization
views.case: The text describes the labor matter of factly and positively, as the necessary means of accomplishing a God-honoring task, rotated humanely, one month away and two months home, verse 14, unlike the harsher forced labor imposed on non-Israelite peoples described later, 1 Kings 9:20-22.

views.label: An early sign of the burdens Solomon's reign would place on the people
views.case: The sheer scale, 30,000 conscripted men plus 150,000 porters and stonecutters, together with Adoniram again named over the forced labor as in chapter 4, anticipates the very complaint Israel raises after Solomon's death, 1 Kings 12:4, that his yoke had grown heavy.

debates.bearing: Either way, the labor accomplishes a genuinely God-honoring task, the preparation of the temple; the second view simply notes that Kings, read as a whole, lets this detail plant a seed that grows into real national grievance by the time of Solomon's son, without diminishing the goodness of the temple's purpose.

study.originalLanguages: Solomon's message turns on his desire to build a bayit, a house, for the shem, the Name, of the LORD his God, verse 3, language drawn from Deuteronomy that locates God's presence and reputation in the place He chooses rather than confining Him within walls. He can undertake this only because the LORD has given him menuchah, rest, on every side, verse 4, precisely the qualification God had already attached to the son who would build His house, 1 Chronicles 22:9. And the resulting arrangement between Solomon and Hiram is sealed as a berit, a treaty, verse 12, the same Hebrew word used throughout Scripture for covenant, so that even this practical trade agreement is described in the vocabulary of binding, faithful relationship.

typology.type: Gentile timber, skill, and labor gathered to build the house of the LORD
typology.fulfillment: The nations themselves gathered as living stones into the true temple, the church, Ephesians 2:19-22, 1 Peter 2:5.

typology.type: Rest given by the LORD as the necessary condition for building God's house
typology.fulfillment: The rest Christ gives His people, upon which He builds His church, Matthew 11:28-29, Matthew 16:18.

typology.type: A house built for the Name of the LORD among His people
typology.fulfillment: Christ, in whom the fullness of God dwells bodily, and who tabernacles among His people, John 1:14, Colossians 2:9.

study.christ: Solomon can build only because the LORD has already given him rest on every side, verse 4, the very qualification promised beforehand for the son who would build God's house, 1 Chronicles 22:9. Jesus is the true Son of David who both grants rest to weary souls, Matthew 11:28-29, and builds His own house, the church, upon the finished work of His cross, Matthew 16:18. And where Hiram, an outsider to Israel, gladly supplies what the temple requires, the New Testament shows the nations themselves, once far off, being built together with God's people into a holy temple in the Lord, Ephesians 2:19-22, the very partnership this chapter quietly foreshadows.
study.god: God is sovereign over international affairs, moving a Gentile king's willing heart to supply exactly what His temple will need, and utterly faithful to His covenant word that David's son, a man given rest, would be the one to build the house for His Name. He is also shown to value patient, costly, unglamorous preparation, years of quarrying and timber-gathering before a single wall of the temple rises, teaching that diligent, careful readiness is itself a fitting act of worship toward Him.

crossReferences.note: The LORD's original promise that David's offspring would build a house for His Name.
David explains to Solomon that he could not build because of his wars and bloodshed, but his son, a man of rest, would build the house.
Jesus offers the rest that Solomon's temple could only foreshadow, rest for the weary soul.
Jew and Gentile alike built together into a holy temple in the Lord, the fulfillment of Gentile partnership in building God's house.

study.oneStory: David longed to build God a house but was denied because of his many wars; here his son, given rest at last by the LORD, gathers Gentile skill, timber, and labor alongside Israel's own strength to prepare everything the temple will need. This is one more step in the long story of God dwelling among His people, from the tabernacle in the wilderness toward the temple in Jerusalem, and ultimately toward Christ, the true and greater temple, in whom God tabernacles among us and gathers a people from every nation into one house built for His Name.

quiz.q: Why, according to Solomon's message in verses 3 to 4, could David not build the house but Solomon now can?

options.text: David faced constant wars on every side, but the LORD has now given Solomon rest on every side, with no adversary or crisis.
options.note: This is the reason Solomon himself states plainly in verses 3-4.

options.text: David was simply too old to undertake such a large building project.
options.note: Age is not the stated reason; the text explicitly names David's wars as the obstacle.

options.text: David did not have enough wealth to fund the temple.
options.note: Wealth is not mentioned as the barrier; the stated reason is the absence of rest from war.

options.text: There was no reason given for the delay; it was simply Solomon's personal preference to build now.
options.note: Solomon gives a clear, specific reason rooted in God's gift of rest, not personal preference.

quiz.q: How does Hiram respond to Solomon's message, and what does his response reveal, verse 7?

options.text: He rejoices greatly and blesses the LORD for giving David a wise son, recognizing God's hand in Solomon's reign even as a foreign king.
options.note: Verse 7 records Hiram's explicit praise of the LORD, an unusual acknowledgment from a Gentile ruler.

options.text: He is mainly interested in the trade benefits of wheat and oil promised in return.
options.note: The exchange of goods is real and follows in verses 8-11, but Hiram's own words in verse 7 focus on blessing the LORD, not the trade terms.

options.text: He refuses at first and must be persuaded through further negotiation.
options.note: The text shows Hiram responding with immediate joy, not initial refusal.

options.text: He shows no reaction at all to Solomon's request.
options.note: Verse 7 explicitly describes Hiram's joyful, worshipful reaction.

quiz.q: What did the exchange between Hiram and Solomon actually involve, verses 8 to 11?

options.text: Hiram supplied cedar and cypress timber floated by sea from Lebanon, and Solomon supplied wheat and olive oil as food for Hiram's household.
options.note: Verses 8-11 detail exactly this mutual exchange of timber for agricultural provisions.

options.text: Hiram supplied only labor, while Solomon paid entirely in silver and gold.
options.note: Silver and gold are not mentioned here; the stated exchange is timber for wheat and oil, plus Israelite labor alongside Hiram's servants.

options.text: Solomon supplied all the timber while Hiram supplied only food.
options.note: The arrangement is reversed from this; Hiram supplied the timber, and Solomon supplied the food.

options.text: There was no real exchange; Hiram simply gave the timber as a free gift.
options.note: The text describes a mutual arrangement, an exchange of goods, not a one-sided gift.

quiz.q: What does the scale of Solomon's labor force, 30,000 conscripted Israelites in rotating shifts plus 150,000 porters and stonecutters, verses 13 to 16, suggest about the temple's preparation?

options.text: Building God's house was a massive, costly, nationwide undertaking, requiring careful and patient preparation long before construction itself began.
options.note: The detailed numbers underscore the scale and seriousness of the preparation described in this chapter.

options.text: It shows only that Solomon enjoyed displaying his personal power through large numbers.
options.note: The numbers are large, but the chapter frames them around preparing the LORD's house, not personal display.

options.text: It shows that the temple was actually a small, modest structure requiring little labor.
options.note: The described labor force is enormous, indicating a major building project, not a modest one.

options.text: It shows that Solomon relied entirely on Hiram's workers and used no Israelite labor.
options.note: Verse 13 explicitly says Solomon conscripted 30,000 men from all Israel.

quiz.q: What is the significance of verse 12, where the LORD gives Solomon wisdom and peace results between Hiram and Solomon, sealed by a treaty?

options.text: It shows God's promised wisdom producing tangible peace and a binding, covenant-like partnership between an Israelite king and a Gentile king.
options.note: The verse directly links God's gift of wisdom to the peaceable treaty that follows, using covenant language, berit, for the agreement.

options.text: It shows that Solomon and Hiram were already close personal friends with no formal agreement needed.
options.note: Hiram was a friend of David, verse 1, but verse 12 specifically describes a formal treaty, not merely informal friendship.

options.text: It shows that the peace between the two kings was temporary and quickly broken.
options.note: The text presents this peace and treaty positively, with no indication in this chapter that it was short-lived.

options.text: It shows that God's wisdom had nothing to do with Solomon's foreign relations.
options.note: The verse explicitly ties God's gift of wisdom to the resulting peace and treaty.

questions: Solomon could only build because the LORD had first given him rest. Where in your life is God asking you to wait for His rest before taking on a new work, rather than forcing it in your own strength?

Hiram, an outsider to Israel's covenant, rejoiced and blessed the LORD when he heard of Solomon's wisdom. Who in your life might see God's hand in you before you expect it, and how can you make that testimony clear?

Building the temple required years of unseen preparation, quarrying stone and gathering timber, before a single wall went up. What patient, unglamorous preparation is God calling you to right now for something you cannot yet see completed?

The treaty between Hiram and Solomon shows God weaving even ordinary trade and diplomacy into His larger purposes. Where do you need to trust that God is at work in your everyday commitments and partnerships, not only in dramatic moments?
This whole chapter exists to prepare a house for the Name of the LORD. What are you preparing your heart or your household for this season, and is it ultimately built for God's name or your own?

translationNotes.note: 'The Sea' in the BSB footnote refers to the Mediterranean Sea, also called the Great Sea, the route by which Hiram's servants floated the cedar and cypress rafts south from Lebanon.

The BSB footnote follows the LXX reading, supported by 2 Chronicles 2:10, of 20,000 baths of pure olive oil; the Hebrew text reads twenty cors of pure or pressed oil, a notable textual variant in quantity.

The BSB footnote observes that forms of the Hebrew berit, translated 'treaty' here, are rendered 'covenant' in most other passages, tying this diplomatic agreement to the same vocabulary Scripture uses for God's covenants with His people.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 5:1-18. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced elsewhere in this file.
